metadata.context: temporal_experience.py confirmed K-rate hierarchy spanning 30 orders. zeno_maxwell.py confirmed four-way equality numerically. This script: Landauer costs across the hierarchy, evolutionary K-efficiency, and the proof that the four-way equality is a logical identity.

section1_landauer_cascade.notes: K-rate 1.0e+13 bits/s per channel at T=310.0 K. Landauer floor 2.967e-08 W/channel. Actual ~4.280e-07 W/channel (~10 k_B T per event). Slack 14.4x — near-quantum events are ~10x above Landauer, far tighter than macroscopic biology.

K-rate 8.60e+20 bits/s total brain. Landauer predicted power: 2.551 W. Actual brain power: 20.0 W. Slack 7.84x — brain runs only 7.8x above Landauer floor for its ion-channel K-processing. This is the tightest slack in any biological system.

K-rate 50 bits/s (integrated conscious bandwidth). Landauer floor 1.483e-19 W — negligible. Actual neural cost for conscious processing ~1.5 W. Slack 1.01e+19x — consciousness runs enormously above its Landauer floor because the neural substrate implementing 50 bits/s carries the full ion-channel K-rate as overhead.

K-rate 2000 bits/s (1000 bp/s × 2 bits/bp). Landauer floor 5.933e-18 W. Actual polymerase power ~4.280e-16 W (~100 k_B T per bp). Slack 72.1x — fidelity enforcement (proofreading) costs ~25x over the Landauer minimum (Hopfield kinetic proofreading).

K-rate 8.333e+05 bits/s (1389 mRNA/s × 300 nt × 2 bits/nt). Landauer floor 2.472e-15 W. Actual RNAP power ~5.944e-13 W. Slack 240.4x — larger than DNA replication because RNAP lacks proofreading (accuracy maintained by other mechanisms).

K-rate 1.1255e-09 bits/s (functional genome K / 3.8 Gyr). Landauer floor 3.339e-30 W — negligible. Actual evolutionary 'dissipation' (Haldane cost) ~2.42e+02 W/lineage. Slack 7.25e+31x — enormous because fitness selection operates across entire organisms, not just bits. Evolutionary K is maximally thermodynamically inefficient at the individual level, but maximally efficient at the information level (K per joule invested).

steps.statement: I(gas; demon) = H(gas) - H(gas | demon) = |ΔH_gas|. The demon's K-acquisition IS the reduction in gas entropy conditional on the demon's memory state. By definition, K_acquired = I(gas; demon). Therefore K_acquired = |ΔH_gas| exactly.

A reversible (ideal) measurement stores exactly I(gas; demon) bits in the demon's memory. The subsequent erasure of the demon's memory erases exactly those bits. No surplus, no deficit. Therefore |ΔH_gas| = K_acquired = bits_erased. Bennett (1982) proved that measurement itself can be done reversibly (no thermodynamic cost) but erasure cannot — and erasure cost = bits stored.

steps.theorem: Landauer's principle (Landauer 1961; Bérut et al. 2012 exp.)
steps.statement: Erasing b bits requires dissipating at least b × k_B T ln(2) joules as heat. This heat increases environment entropy by b × k_B ln(2) J/K = b bits (in natural information units with k_B ln(2) per bit). Therefore bits_erased = ΔS_environment exactly at the Landauer limit. Real processes exceed the Landauer minimum, so ΔS_env ≥ bits_erased, with equality only at the thermodynamic limit.

section3_K_conservation_proof.conservation_statement: K_acquired ≡ |ΔH_gas| ≡ bits_erased ≡ ΔS_environment (the K-change conservation law). K-information acquired from a system increases the entropy of the environment by exactly the same amount. K is not destroyed — it is transferred from the measured system to the environment. The total K-entropy of (system + environment) is conserved.
section3_K_conservation_proof.physical_interpretation: To KNOW something costs entropy. Szilard (1929) showed that the Szilard engine extracts W = k_B T ln(2) of work per bit of knowledge. Landauer (1961) showed that erasing that bit dumps the same energy as heat. Bennett (1982) completed the picture: reversible measurement is free, but the ledger must balance at erasure. The four-way equality is the bookkeeping identity for this ledger: K acquired from gas = K stored in demon = K erased = K dumped to environment.
key_references: Szilard L. (1929) Z. Phys. 53:840 — K-acquisition from thermodynamic systems
Shannon C.E. (1948) Bell Syst. Tech. J. — mutual information I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y)
Landauer R. (1961) IBM J. Res. Dev. — erasure of 1 bit costs k_B T ln(2)
Bennett C.H. (1982) Int. J. Theor. Phys. — reversible measurement + erasure analysis
Bérut A. et al. (2012) Nature 483:187 — experimental verification of Landauer's principle
